Parameter: Counterfactual mortality rate among women with obstetric hemorrhage
Source / notes: RCT control group mortality 2.3% (Miller et al. 2013), adjusted down 31% for country-level maternal hemorrhage mortality reductions between 2010-2019 (GBD). Result: 1.6%.
Pooled RR 0.52 (95% CI 0.36-0.77) from random effects meta-analysis of 5 observational/pre-post studies (Pileggi-Castro et al. 2015 systematic review).

Evidence is mostly observational (4 pre/post + 1 fully observational study). One underpowered RCT (OR 0.36, NS). Potential confounding from differences in care quality between study phases. Original QEA applied 66%.
Implementation likely better quality in study settings than real-world. Scale-up data from Zimbabwe (90% usage) is encouraging. Original QEA applied 75%.
Calculation: 1.6% mortality × 24% adjusted reduction = ~0.38%

Parameter: Units of value per maternal death averted (GW moral weights)
Source / notes: GiveWell moral weights for adult female death at average age 27-30. Used in original BOTEC.

Parameter: UoV from mortality per woman treated
Source / notes: Calculation
I'm assuming +15%. NASG reduces severe anemia at discharge and speeds recovery from shock (165 min vs 209 min in RCT). Also reduces need for emergency hysterectomy. Rough guess.

Parameter: Infant mortality uplift (% of mortality UoV)
Source / notes: I'm assuming +10%. Maternal death during/after delivery substantially increases infant mortality. Observational evidence suggests 4-25x higher infant mortality when mothers die. This is a rough guess — not formally modeled.

Parameter: Total UoV per woman treated (with adjustments)
Source / notes: Calculation: mortality UoV × (1 + morbidity + infant)
Standard GW hypothetical grant

Parameter: Cost per woman treated — best guess ($)
Source / notes: I'm assuming $12 as midpoint. Device cost ~$1/use (amortized over 72 uses). Training ~$1.62/use. Additional logistics, supervision, return-and-exchange system. Original QEA guessed $8; CHAI Zimbabwe suggests higher total costs when including broader programmatic activities.

Original BOTEC noted mean treatment cost of $54.56/woman across both RCT groups (including blood transfusion, uterotonics). This would represent NASG as part of full hemorrhage treatment, not incremental NASG cost.

Parameter: 1. Mortality rate uses RCT control group
Source / notes: The 1.6% counterfactual mortality is based on the RCT control group in Zambia/Zimbabwe, adjusted for time trends. Actual mortality among hemorrhaging women varies substantially by setting (higher in Nigeria, lower in India).

Parameter: 2. NASG reaches all hemorrhaging women at facilities
Source / notes: BOTEC assumes 100% coverage of women with obstetric hemorrhage at participating facilities. Zimbabwe pilot achieved 90%. Women hemorrhaging before facility arrival are not covered.

The pooled RR 0.52 comes from non-experimental studies. The 50% IV/EV adjustment attempts to account for this, but the true effect is uncertain. The RCT point estimate (OR 0.36) is actually larger but is not significant (n=15 deaths).

Parameter: 4. Cost per woman excludes ART-like ongoing costs
Source / notes: Unlike HIV interventions, NASG is a one-time device application with no ongoing treatment costs. The garment itself is reusable. This makes the cost-effectiveness calculation simpler but the upfront cost ($57-100/garment) must be amortized over actual (not theoretical) reuses.
